title: How to build a life that does not require armor
author: Kalesh

metaDescription:

htmlContent:
What we mistake for strength

I built my armor piece by piece. Every betrayal added a plate. Every humiliation welded a seam. Every moment I showed my softness and got punished for it, I reinforced the structure. By the time I was twenty-five, I could walk through the world without feeling much of anything. That was the point, right? To be untouchable. To be so protected that nothing could get in.

Here's the thing. Armor works. It does exactly what it's designed to do. It keeps danger out. It makes you look strong. It lets you function in environments that would destroy someone who was open and soft and alive. I got promotions. I got respect. I got told I was "so together" and "so resilient" and all those other compliments we give to people who have learned to hide their bleeding.

But armor also keeps you trapped inside yourself. It insulates you from pain, sure. But it also insulates you from connection, from joy, from the kind of spontaneous aliveness that children have before they learn that the world will hurt them. You don't get to selectively numb. You numb everything. That's the deal.

Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed polyvagal theory, talks about how our nervous system is constantly scanning for safety and danger. When we live in armor, we're telling our system that danger is everywhere. We're bracing. We're preparing. We're never fully in our bodies because being in our bodies means feeling what's actually there. And what's actually there, for many of us, is a lot of pain.

So we stay up in our heads. We stay busy. We stay in control. We build lives that look good from the outside while the inside is a hollowed out shell. And then we wonder why we feel empty.

What happens when you start to put it down

I didn't decide to take my armor off all at once. Nobody does that. You can't. The armor is there for a reason. It saved your life. It got you through. Taking it off too fast can be destabilizing, even dangerous. Your nervous system needs to know that there's something safe underneath before it will let go of its protection.

What happened instead was that I started noticing. I noticed when my shoulders were up by my ears. I noticed when my jaw was clenched. I noticed when I was holding my breath. I noticed when I was dissociating during conversations, going through the motions while my body was somewhere else entirely. Just noticing. Not trying to change anything. Just being curious about what my armor felt like from the inside.

This is where Peter Levine's work on somatic experiencing becomes relevant. Levine, the founder of Somatic Experiencing, understood that trauma lives in the body. It's not just a story in your head. It's a physiological response that gets stuck. The armor you wear is the physical ation of that stuckness. It's your body saying, "I'm still in danger. I need to stay ready."

So the first step in building a life without armor isn't to rip it off. It's to start noticing what it feels like to wear it. To be curious about which parts of your body hold tension. To track what happens when you feel threatened. To begin to differentiate between a real threat in the present moment and a memory of threat that your nervous system hasn't finished processing.

I started doing this practice where I would pause several times a day and just feel my feet on the floor. That's it. Just feet on floor. I would notice how my weight distributed. I would notice if I was leaning forward, ready to fight or flee, or if I could let my weight drop down into my heels. Stay with me here. This sounds simple. It's not simple. For someone who has been living in their head for decades, coming back into the body can feel terrifying. But it's also the only way out.

The cost of staying armored

Let me be direct about something. The wellness industry has sold us a fantasy about healing. They tell you that if you just meditate enough, journal enough, do enough yoga, you'll transcend your pain. They tell you that you can "release" your trauma in a weekend workshop. They tell you that healing is linear and beautiful and that you'll emerge like a butterfly from a cocoon.

Bullshit.

Healing is ugly. It's messy. It takes years. And it never ends. You don't arrive at some final destination where you're "healed" and you never have to deal with your shit again. You just get better at noticing when your armor is up. You get better at choosing, sometimes, to put it down. You get better at tolerating the vulnerability of being seen.

I'm not saying this to discourage you. I'm saying it because the fantasy of perfect healing keeps people stuck. If you believe that you should be further along than you are, you'll judge yourself for still having armor. And that judgment just adds another layer of protection. Shame makes armor thicker. Always.

A gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection is Radical Acceptance (paid link). Tara Brach's work on radical acceptance offers a way to meet your armor with compassion rather than more criticism. It's not about getting rid of the armor. It's about making friends with the part of you that needed it. That's a very different project.

The cost of staying armored is real. You lose connection. You lose intimacy. You lose the ability to be truly seen by another person because you're always performing strength. You lose access to your own body. You lose the capacity for deep pleasure because deep pleasure requires the same openness as deep pain. You can't have one without the other. Armor blocks both.

Building a life that doesn't require armor

I want to be careful here. I'm not saying you should walk around unprotected all the time. That's not realistic. There are situations where armor is appropriate. There are people who have not earned the right to see your softness. There are environments that are genuinely dangerous. I'm not asking you to be naive.

What I'm asking is this: Can you build a life where you don't have to wear armor all the time? Can you create relationships, work, and living situations where it's safe enough to let your guard down sometimes? Can you start to trust that you can handle what comes, not because you're armored, but because you're resilient?

A compassionate reframing of trauma that resonates with many is What Happened to You? (paid link) by Bruce Perry and Oprah Winfrey. The title itself is the shift. Instead of "what's wrong with you," which is the question that keeps armor in place, we ask "what happened to you." That question opens the door to understanding. And understanding is the beginning of softening.

I think about Richard Schwartz's Internal Family Systems model a lot in this context. Schwartz talks about how every part of us has a positive intention, even the parts that seem problematic. Your armor is a part. It's a protector. It's doing its job. It's trying to keep you safe. When you fight your armor, it just digs in deeper. When you thank it for its service and ask what it needs, something shifts. The armor starts to relax, just a little.

A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate is No Bad Parts (paid link). Schwartz's book walks you through the process of getting to know your inner system. It's a roadmap for making peace with the parts that keep you armored.

Building a life without armor means building a life where you can be honest about your limits. It means saying no when you need to. It means asking for help. It means letting people see you struggle. It means admitting that you're scared or sad or angry instead of pretending you're fine. It means trusting that you are worthy of care even when you're not performing strength.

This is terrifying for people who have been armored their whole lives. I know. I've been there. The idea of putting down the armor feels like death. It feels like you'll be annihilated. And in a way, you will be. The self that you built to survive will have to die so that a more can live. That's grief work. That's real.

One of the foundational texts on trauma recovery is Trauma and Recovery (paid link) by Judith Herman. Herman's work makes clear that recovery happens in the context of relationship. You cannot heal in isolation. You need safe others who can witness your process without needing you to be okay. That's the container in which armor can be set down.

The ongoing practice

I still wear armor. I'm wearing it right now, writing this. I can feel the tension in my shoulders. I can feel the way I'm bracing against your judgment. I can feel the part of me that wants to sound smart and together and like I have this all figured out. That's the armor. It's still here. It's not gone.

But I can notice it now. That's the difference. Before, I was the armor. I didn't know there was anything else. Now I know that underneath the armor there is a person who is scared and tender and wants to be loved. And I know that person is worth protecting, not with walls, but with discernment. Not with numbness, but with boundaries. Not with performance, but with presence.

Wild, right? The opposite of armor isn't weakness. It's presence. It's the capacity to be here, in your body, in this moment, without needing to be anywhere else. It's the ability to feel your feelings without being consumed by them. It's the willingness to be seen, even when being seen is terrifying.

I don't know what your armor looks like. Maybe it's intellectualizing. Maybe it's people-pleasing. Maybe it's anger. Maybe it's perfectionism. Maybe it's addiction. Maybe it's staying so busy that you never have to sit still. The shape doesn't matter as much as the function. The function is protection. And protection was necessary. It was a gift you gave yourself when no one else was protecting you.

But you're not that helpless child anymore. You have resources now that you didn't have then. You have choices. You have agency. You have the capacity to build a life that doesn't require constant vigilance. A life where you can rest. A life where you can be soft. A life where you can trust that even if something goes wrong, you'll be able to handle it, not because you're armored, but because you're alive and resourceful and connected to others.

That's the return. That's what this category is about. Returning to yourself. Returning to your body. Returning to the possibility of a life that isn't organized around survival. It's not easy. It's not linear. It's not something you accomplish once and then you're done. It's a practice. A daily, sometimes hourly, practice of noticing when the armor comes up and choosing, when it's safe, to let it down.

I'm still practicing. I'll be practicing for the rest of my life. And honestly? That's okay. That's more than okay. That's what it means to be human.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Radical Acceptance (paid link) - Tara Brach's gentle approach to working with shame and self-rejection
  • What Happened to You? (paid link) - Bruce Perry and Oprah Winfrey's compassionate reframing of trauma
  • No Bad Parts (paid link) - Richard Schwartz's gentle introduction to parts work
  • Trauma and Recovery (paid link) - Judith Herman's foundational text on trauma recovery
